long_candidates.reason: 4 politicians buying MSFT: Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, Mcguire

decisions.reasoning: Strongest signal in the dataset: 4 politicians buying across both chambers (Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, McGuire). Cluster of 4 is meaningful — spread across Senate and House, different political affiliations, trades spanning April–May 2026. Warner sits on Senate Intelligence (tech oversight), adding committee relevance. Trade sizes are small ($1K–$15K) which is typical for political disclosures and doesn't dilute the signal. MSFT is in a secular AI/cloud uptrend, which supports the long bias.
decisions.key_risk: Small trade sizes across all 4 actors suggest these could be routine diversified purchases rather than conviction insider signals. Cluster may simply reflect public AI/cloud enthusiasm rather than non-public information.

decisions.reasoning: Two politicians buying (Fetterman, Keating) in mid-May 2026. Keating sits on the House Foreign Affairs Committee — limited direct banking oversight. Fetterman has no primary banking committee role. The cluster is only 2 actors with minimal committee relevance, and financials have been broadly recovering. Weak but present signal.
decisions.key_risk: No strong committee oversight connection to banking sector. Could easily be a reaction to public macro/rate news rather than inside information. Low conviction.

decisions.reasoning: Two senators buying GOOGL (Boozman, Fetterman) with trades in April–May 2026. Boozman sits on the Senate Appropriations Committee with tech spending visibility; Fetterman's buy is concurrent with his MSFT purchase suggesting a broad tech long bet. GOOGL is in an AI tailwind and antitrust overhang has modestly lifted. Signal is modest but directionally consistent.
decisions.key_risk: Only 2 actors, small amounts. Boozman and Fetterman also bought MSFT simultaneously — this may be a broad diversified tech basket purchase rather than GOOGL-specific conviction. Ongoing DOJ antitrust risk remains.

decisions.reasoning: Two senators buying Constellation Energy (Boozman, Warner) in early May 2026. CEG is a nuclear energy pure-play benefiting from AI data center power demand and federal clean energy policy. Warner sits on the Senate Finance and Intelligence Committees with energy policy exposure; Boozman on Appropriations. Nuclear energy has strong policy tailwinds and this is a relatively concentrated, less obvious pick — less likely to be a purely public-news-driven buy compared to MSFT/AAPL.
decisions.key_risk: Only 2 actors. CEG has had a strong run — entering now risks buying near a peak. Nuclear permitting/regulatory setbacks could reverse momentum quickly.

decisions.reasoning: Two politicians buying AAPL (Warner, McGuire). However, AAPL is one of the most widely held stocks in the country — politicians buying small amounts is extremely commonplace and has very low signal value. No specific committee oversight linkage that would suggest privileged information. The signal is too generic to act on with conviction.
decisions.key_risk: Near-zero informational edge. AAPL purchases by politicians are so routine they are essentially noise. Tariff/China supply chain risk remains an ongoing headwind.

decisions.reasoning: Two politicians selling AT&T (Fetterman, Gottheimer). However, only 2 actors — well below the 3+ threshold for meaningful short conviction. Neither sits on a telecom-specific oversight committee with clear informational advantage. T is a high-dividend, slow-growth stock and sells could simply reflect portfolio rebalancing or profit-taking after a recent yield-driven rally. Short signal is too weak to act on. Shorting T also offers limited upside given its already-compressed valuation.
decisions.key_risk: Unlimited short downside on a defensive dividend stock. Insufficient conviction from only 2 sellers with no strong committee link. Risk of a short squeeze if rates drop and dividend stocks re-rate.

decisions.reasoning: Two House members selling AMT (Evans, McCormick). McCormick sits on the House Armed Services Committee — no meaningful telecom tower/REIT oversight. Evans is on Ways & Means — also no direct AMT relevance. With only 2 sellers, no committee linkage, and small trade sizes, this is a very weak short signal. AMT REITs are rate-sensitive and the sells may simply reflect rising rate concerns that are fully public. Not enough conviction to open a short.
decisions.key_risk: Insufficient signal quality. Only 2 actors, no committee oversight connection. AMT could re-rate sharply if interest rates decline, causing significant short losses.
